[[RunningHead]]

Two Classes of Factors Affecting Dream Recall

Edward Wolpert M.D.

Edward Wolpert

Edward A. Wolpert, M.D.
Michael Reese Hospital and Medical Center
2959 South Ellis Avenue, Chicago, Ill. 60616

Edward Wolpert  

SUMMARY

Dream recall was seen to be determined by two classes of factors. The first included four classical faculty psychology concepts: the "laws" of consolidation, stimulus characteristics, decay, and awakening characteristics. Such "laws" indicate that the prerequisites for the psychological act of dream recall include biologically giveen limitations: The dream presentation must be registered or consolidated for a finite time period (Law of Consolidation). The consolidation must occur within a certain period after the dream ceased (Law of Decay). There are externally imposed limitations on what can be recalled from a series (Law of Stimulus Characteristics). Remembering depends in part on how the stimulus is

56

interrupted (Law of Awakening Characteristics). Failure of such "laws" to account for empirical observations was attributed to a second class of factors described by Freud as repression. The second prerequisite for the psychological act of dream recall is determined by the emotional needs of the dreamer. Ideational representations of unacceptable impulses will be hidden from the dreamer both by being disguised in the dream content and by the entire dream not being available to consciousness. Undoing of repression of visual elements of dreams depends on subtle or not so subtle shifts in transference meaning of the analyst along both narcissistic and object relation parameters. Any complete account of the remembering and forgetting of dreams must include both classes of factors: utilization of one set of factors without the other is incomplete. Memory thus is determined both by the limitations imposed on the nervous system by its biological structure and by psychological conflict interfering with its function. Because the absence of dreaming never occurs, the presence of forgetting may or may not indicate current psychological conflict.

Continuing Research: The Modification of Defenses in Psychoanalysis

Jules Weiss M.D.

Jules Weiss

Jules Weiss, M.D.
2420 Sutter Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Jules Weiss  

Emanuel Windholz introduced the report, which was based on a research project1 concerning changes in the defenses of the ego during psychoanalysis. He briefly described the history of the project, which grew out of a purely clinical study of the analysis of a patient with an obsessive-compulsive character disorder. The study provided some preliminary formulations about the patient's defenses and how they changed during the course of the analysis; the research grew out of an attempt to extend these findings and to evaluate them.

Joseph Weiss presented a paper, "The Emergence of New Themes in Analysis: A Contribution to the Psychoanalytic Theory of Therapy," opening with the question: How does a patient become able to bring forth a previously warded-off mental content? A basic theoretical idea that has guided the group's thinking about this question is that the ego plays a predominant role in this process, ordinarily bringing warded-off contents to the surface only when it is safe to do so. This idea helps to account for the observation that the neurotic patient is not ordinarily overwhelmed, and hence traumatized, by the emergence of warded-off contents.

It is only in unusual circumstances in the analysis of a neurotic patient that the warded-off impulse overwhelms the ego defense and breaks through to the surface. When this does occur, the emergence of the impulse is more apt to be traumatic and disruptive than therapeutic. The patient is apt to rerepress the impulse as soon as he can, or, if he cannot repress it, he may retain it in consciousness as part of a symptom; or he may act it out.

Emanuel Windholz  

MY AIM IN THIS PRESENTATION IS TO DESCRIBE the experiences of different Study Groups of COPE, to inform you of their methods of investigation, and particularly the problems they met and the solutions they employed. It is my hope that this review will provide some perspectives for an evaluation of the problems that face us today, as was true of the review of the first two years of COPE by Bonnett (1962).

COPE'S first Study Group explored the topic of curriculum. A conflict of interest, to be repeated many times in the history of COPE, between long-range studies and action-directed recommendations was immediately evident. A second conflict, somewhat related to the first, appeared later between investigation of the topic of curriculum by members of the Study Group alone as opposed to seeking information (as one of several goals) via conferences with representatives of institutes. Over the years the different Study Groups of COPE have made compromises regarding these two central conflicts, with the general emphasis being in favor of long-range studies by members of Study Groups working alone.

Summary

Kenneth Calder M.D.

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth T. Calder, M.D.
110 East 78 Street
New York, New York 10021

Kenneth Calder  

AS YOU DOUBTLESS HAVE GATHERED from the preceding papers, the essential orientation of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education is to provide a service or services for the American Psychoanalytic Association. What has been done by COPE has been emphasized by Fleming, Arlow, Windholz, and myself. What may be done and perhaps should be done in the future is suggested by Pollock and Wallerstein. The American uses the findings of COPE to help candidates (e.g., better selection); or psychoanalysts (e.g., better knowledge of how to select); or the community (e.g., better analyses because selection of psychoanalysts has improved). Although the special domain of COPE is psychoanalytic education, it is clear that the impact of its conclusions can be widespread.

My belief is that there are four educational services that could be provided by our national association for our members and institutes: (1) research on educational topics; (2) education in educational matters: of members, institutes, or other units of the Association; (3) integration or coordination: to provide overall perspective of what goes on in psychoanalytic education in our Association and component parts so that, at the least, everyone is informed; and (4) standard setting and/or monitoring.

The first, research on educational topics, is what COPE has emphasized. Concentration on this service removes COPE from the pressures of time and action and, potentially at least, encourages more basic or more general or more profound conclusions.

Submitted May 31, 1972

607

The Stork Fable—Some Psychodynamic Considerations

Marvin Margolis Ph.D. and Philip Parker M.D.

Philip Parker

Marvin Margolis, Ph.D.
Philip Parker, M.D.
Detroit Psychiatric Institute
1151 Taylor
Detroit, Michigan 48202

Philip Parker  

SUMMARY

Impressed by the popularity and persistence of the stork fable, we have traced its history through the mythology of many cultures. We have noted the discrepancies and similarities between the stork of the fable and the real bird. We have offered an explanation for its survival, based on the psychological needs of both children and their parents.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Amphetamine in a Case of Hysteria

Leon Berman M.D.

Leon Berman

Leon E.A. Berman, M.D.
6440 Hills Drive
Birmingham, Mich. 48010

Leon Berman  

SUMMARY

A young woman suffering from a hysterical neurosis confessed, after a year and a half of analysis, her dependency on amphetamine diet pills. The symbolic meaning of the drug to the patient, and the role it played in her symptom complex have been discussed.

The pill symbolically expressed both the phallic and oral elements of her neurosis. It represented her illusory penis and

339

defended against feelings of genital damage. It also represented emergency food supplies and defended against feelings of oral abandonment. In providing a sense of phallic wholeness to reassure her against the illusion of genital defect, it functioned as a fetish. As an emergency narcissistic supply to relieve her feelings of separation anxiety, it served as a transitional object.

Finally, the pharmacologic effect of the amphetamine helped to regulate her feelings of self-esteem and to provide a sense of narcissistic power.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Defense Mechanism: A Survey and Synthesis

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D. and Joseph Slap M.D.

Joseph Slap

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Joseph William Slap, M.D.
1616 18th Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20009

Joseph Slap  

SUMMARY

We have selected from the current theory of defense mechanisms those concepts which seemed most valid to us from the standpoints of child observation and clinical experience. The hypothesis that defense mechanisms are cognitive structures is presented and applied to the development of individual defense mechanisms. We have also taken up the concept of intersystemic suborganizations, and discussed the role defense mechanisms play in these structures. Lastly, we have noted that in the course of successful psychoanalysis, when the suborganizations are exposed and the conflicts worked through, the defensive operations tend to lose their primitive character and to take on the more refined and adaptive qualities of the cognitive functions of which they are examples.

[[RunningHead]]

What Do We Face and where can We Go?: Questions about Future Directions

George Pollock M.D.

George Pollock

George H. Pollock, M.D.
180 No. Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 6060

George Pollock  

THE COMMITEE ON PSYCHOANALYTIC EDUCATION'S Plenary Planning Group asked me to talk about future directions in psychoanalytic education as related to medicine and psychiatry. Though timely, this is a risky business, as is any forecasting. Everything seems to be in a state of transition, and no one knows where we will be 10 years from today. What we do recognize is that we cannot isolate ourselves from the tensions and currents of today's world; and yet, we must maintain what has proved valuable and valid in the past and in the present. Nevertheless, the voices of the past may not always be the voices of the present or of the future. The world changes, nor is psychoanalysis the same as it once was.

COPE, although contributing valuable information to psychoanalysis, is not an action committee. Its charge is to study and investigate various topics and areas, with no time limit, and it has taken as long as it has needed for its researches. In some instances the hope of COPE is to stimulate others to carry on needed studies; like St. Thomas Aquinas, COPE feels "It is not necessary that whosoever sees a mirror should see all that is in a mirror." Nonetheless we now have urgent needs, and we may not have time only for scholarly researches but may require, in addition, actionoriented investigations and action itself. In what will follow, I hope to pinpoint some current trends that require attention.

Current Perspectives and Issues

It was Samuel Johnson who said "The business of life is to go forward.

Submitted May 31, 1972

574

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE DISCOVERY OF THE UNCONSCIOUS: THE HISTORY AND EVOLUTION OF DYNAMIC PSYCHIATRY. By Henri F. Ellenberger. New York: Basic Books, 1970, 932 pp., $15.00.

Professor Ellenberger's book relates the history of the rise of the psychological approach to the understanding and treatment of psychiatric disturbances, giving equal emphasis to the work of Janet, Freud, Adler, and Jung. A background survey of the psychotherapeutic systems of primitive medicine, those of the philosophical schools of antiquity, and that of Christian religious healing is followed by an account of the pioneering efforts of Mesmer and Puységur. Psychotherapy fell into disrepute during the Positivist 19th century but its tradition was revived by Liébeault and Bernheim at Nancy and by Charcot in Paris. This "First Dynamic Psychiatry" was centered on hypnotism. Ellenberger reviews the social, politico-economic, and cultural conditions of this era which are usually designated as Romanticism. The work of Fechner and Bachofen is the most characteristic manifestation of this movement within psychology.

The "New Dynamic Psychiatry" arose at the inception of the modern era, and its intellectual founder was Nietzsche. In presenting an account of the lives and backgrounds of the four protagonists of the new psychiatry, Ellenberger appraises their personalities, compares them with some of their principal contemporaries, outlines the intellectual content of their oeuvres, and estimates the sources of their ideas and their subsequent influence.

Psychoanalysts may be most interested in Ellenberger's 153-page essay on Freud which utilizes information brought to light since publication of the Jones biography and calls into question certain accepted assumptions about Freud. The author's most original interpretation is that of Freud's neurotic symptoms as a "creative illness" from which he emerged, through his self-analysis, as a wise man and a hero. Another novel idea is that the psychoanalytic movement constitutes the revival of the organizational structure of the philosophical schools of antiquity.

Psychoanalytic Education—Editor's Note

J. F.

J. F.

J. F.  

The ever-increasing concern with psychoanalytic education by the American Psychoanalytic Association is attested to by the extensive activities of the Board on Professional Standards. Upon completion of his term as Chairman of the Committee on Institutes, Dr. Sylvan Keiser presented a report whose implications were felt to be of significance for the membership of the Association. Furthermore, the self-scrutiny, which is one of the many functions of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education, culminated in a panel surveying its work for the past 10 years, with a look ahead into the future suggesting some directions which psychoanalytic education might take in the light of its own experiences, as well as the changing scene of psychoanalytic education in general. All of this, together with an article on assessing the analyzability of potential patients for candidates undergoing supervision, a procedure in which the candidates participated, led the Editorial Board to suggest a section on Psychoanalytic Education incorporating these contributions.

"Thank You"

Gert Heilbrunn M.D.

Gert Heilbrunn

Gert Heilbrunn, M.D.
1116 Summit Avenue
Seattle, Washington 98101

Gert Heilbrunn  

SUMMARY

The expression of gratitude maintains a dynamic equilibrium between donor and receiver. Gratitude has the function of a reciprocal gift which symbolizes the extension of a quantum of affection and thus reverses the roles of donor and receiver. Through word or action the primary donor offers a quantity of love. Its acceptance affords him vicarious enjoyment through identification with the receiver; its acknowledgement provides satisfaction of his dependent needs. Both signal possible future repetition of the process with the proven love object. Delay or omission of grateful acknowledgement is equated with rejection, and interrupts the cherished stability of a mutually appetitive relationship which follows the model of the original, affective mother-infant dialogue. Anxiety, depression, anger, and guilt ensue and bring into focus the aversive components of the ambivalent feelings toward the rejecting source. The failure of the patency of giving as an instrument or purposeful action and of reaction formation exposes the donor to his unacceptable, hostile, and dependent impulses.

Symbols in Shadows—A Study of Shadows in Dreams

Charles Sarnoff M.D.

Charles Sarnoff

Charles A. Sarnoff, M.D.
201 Myrtle Drive
Great Neck, New York 10021

Charles Sarnoff  

SUMMARY

The use of shadows as symbols in art, stories, folklore, and dreams has been surveyed. Shadows are rare as symbols in art and equally rare in dreams. In fact, they fail as symbols in dreams. The potential for multiple representations implicit in the visual imagery of the shadow defeats the goals of psychoanalytic symbol formation. The shadow as a dream symbol reveals what it should conceal. Where the purpose of a symbol is the masking of meaning, shadows can serve no better than a sieve can serve as a parasol.

Book Notices

THE WRITINGS OF ANNA FREUD. Volume V, 1956-1965. Research at the Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic and Other Papers. New York: International Universities Press, 1969, xii + 575 pp., $7 50.

Part I of the fifth volume of this compilation is addressed to fund-granting foundations. It describes Anna Freud's methodology, documenting the first steps leading to her creation of a new clinical tool—the Developmental Profile—and contains an account of new Conceptualization-Developmental Lines. In this section, too, are a "Metapsychological Assessment of the Adult Personality—The Adult Profile," an account of the manner in which pathology in childhood is assessed, the relationship between psychoanalysis and the law, and a description of services for underprivileged children. Part II takes up direct child observation, prediction, adolescence, comments on Bowlby's controversial views regarding separation, and numerous published discussions on parent-child relationships, obsessional neurosis, plus tributes to Ernst Kris and Herman Nunberg. Various applications of psychoanalytic thinking to child upbringing, child guidance clinics, nursery schools, emotional and social development of children, children in hospitals, law and pediatric practice take up Part III. Part IV consists of prefaces, introductions, forewords and tributes to papers and books by Casuso, Kata Levy, Nagera, Milner, Freeman et al. Part V is a separate item, consisting of Anna Freud's address upon receiving the Honorary Doctor of Science Award from Jefferson Medical College in 1964.

THE WRITINGS OF ANNA FREUD. Volume VII, 1966-1970. Problems of Psychoanalytic Training, Diagnosis and the Technique of Therapy. New York: International Universities Press, 1971, xi + 312 pp., $12.50.

Here are Anna Freud's latest papers, which carry on her well-known ideas on developmental phenomena, diagnosis, technique, and practical applications in the areas of education, law, guidance clinics, the special problems of underprivileged and handicapped children, etc. Great emphasis in this volume, however, is placed on questions of the training and education of psychoanalyst, both child and adult, and the "difficulties which beset the members of the International Psycho-Analytical Association at the present time.

Superego Aspects of Free Association and the Fundamental Rule

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
120 East 36th St.
New York, N.Y. 10016

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

The superego was not only the last of the personality systems to be introduced into psychoanalysis—its actual differentiation in descriptions of the analytic process has remained the most sketchy. The serious consequences for a balanced view of the therapeutic

264

procedure, one that includes progressive and adaptive as well as infantile trends, are cited.

In correspondingly reassessing the analytic method, the fundamental rule is contemplated in its determining effects upon "free association." The imperative and idealizing aspects of the former are contrasted with the supposedly unfettered course of the latter. Where free association predisposes to withdrawal and narcissistic states, the fundamental rule involves a two-person relationship with historical and functional antecedents in hypnosis. Verbalization, the impartment of insight, and the guilt and confessional aspects of "resistances to be overcome" not only have superego significance but also recognizable forebears in the most ancient forms of psychotherapy as well as the child-parent relationship.

The "normal ego," defined as the patient's compliance with the fundamental rule, is found to contain within it a "normal superego." Complementary considerations enter into the exercise of the analyst's role as auxiliary superego. Where Lewin (1955) once perceptively defined the analyst as the (external) "guardian of free association" (p. 189), a modernization of old guidelines will recognize that the patient himself is the inner guardian.

Bases for Judgments of Analyzability

Richard Lower M.D. , Philip Escoll M.D. and Howard Huxster M.D.

Howard Huxster

Richard B. Lower, M.D.
Philip J. Escoll, M.D.
Howard K. Huxster, M.D.
430 Morris Avenue
Bryn Mawr, Pa. 19010

Howard Huxster  

SUMMARY

A study of recommendations and decisions made in the evaluation of 40 applicants for psychoanalysis allows us to draw the following conclusions:

1. Screening analysts tended to respond to applicants more favorably than did the analyzability committee. Two-thirds of all qualities mentioned by screening analysts were favorable for analyzability, while only one-third of qualities mentioned by the committee were favorable. In like manner, screening analysts recommended two-thirds of the applicants for analysis, while the committee accepted only one-third.

2. Screening analysts tended to use subjective terms in their favorable recommendations, whereas the committee tended to use more objective terms. In unfavorable recommendations, however, both screening analysts and the committee tended toward objectivity.

3. Main favorable qualities in rank order of mention by screening analysts were: psychological mindedness, motivation, favorable subjective response, ego strength, lack of defensiveness.

Main favorable qualities in rank order of mention by the committee were: oedipal pathology, good social adaptation, good work performance, ego strength, mature motivation.

4. Main unfavorable qualities in rank order of mention by screening analysts were: poor ego strength, poor social adaptation, preoedipal pathology, defensiveness.

Main unfavorable qualities in rank order of mention by the committee were: poor ego strength, preoedipal pathology, poor social adaptation, defensiveness.

Time in Psychoanalytic Technique

Alfredo Namnum M.D.

Alfredo Namnum

Alfredo Namnum, M.D.
Paseo de la Reforma 240
Mexico 10, D. F.

Alfredo Namnum  

SUMMARY

Because it aims at correcting the effects of past experience, the most basic objective of the psychoanalytic procedure is to actualize the past. This is partly achieved by the manipulation of time experience, which in analysis is twofold: by promoting the denial of the idea of time in its own setting ("timelessness"), it brings forth the past, and by inducing time consciousness through a strict control of rigid time arrangements, it emphasizes current reality. Under these contrasting conditions, the experiencing of the interaction of the past and the present is facilitated. We have explored some implications of these observations for a psychology of time. We have also attempted to view the question of timing in analysis in relation to the genesis of the time experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Indications and Contraindications for the Psychoanalysis of the Adolescent

Morris Sklansky M.D.

Morris Sklansky

Morris A. Sklansky, M.D.
180 No. Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 60601

Morris Sklansky  

Psychoanalysis has brought vast understanding to the complex psychology of the adolescent. It was evident from this panel presentation that the complexity of adolescent psychology makes the task of evaluating the analyzability of adolescents a difficult one; that, indeed, few contemporary adolescent patients are analyzable; that once in analysis, a variety of parameters of technique far beyond those used in the classical analysis of adults are necessary.

J. Cotter Hirschberg introduced the panel by citing Ralph Greenson who, in discussing Freud's distinction between transference neuroses and narcissistic neuroses with regard to their suitability and unsuitability for analysis, was led to a series of questions about analyzability. Hirschberg applied Greenson's questions to adolescent patients. Is their capacity for object relations enough for them to be able to tolerate the vicissitudes of transference phenomena? Can the adolescent develop a working alliance with observing as well as experiencing ego? Are his synthetic and integrative ego functions enough for working through while maintaining the complex relationship necessary in analysis?

Berman reminded us that the expansion of psychoanalytic theory has widened the scope of treatment from adults to children and now to adolescents. This panel is the first to give scientific consideration specifically to psychoanalysis as a method of treatment for adolescent disturbances. He felt that assessment of analyzability for adults differs from that for adolescents.

Held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, D.C., May 1, 1971. Chairman: J. Cotter Hirschberg.

Submitted August 28, 1971

SUMMARY

The impact on the adult of childhood sibling loss is a significant contributor to later adult psychopathology. It may have played a role in the life of Bertha Pappenheim and her subsequent historical disorder. An attempt has been made to present pertinent historical data which have been recovered by the author in order to provide evidence for his hypothesis. The interpretation of the case of Anna O. in the light of this presentation presents an additional perspective to this case.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Dilemmas in Psychoanalytic Education

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow, M.D.
120 West 59th Street
New York, New York, 10019

Jacob Arlow  

I AM INDEBTED TO DR. FLEMING FOR REMINDING ME of the quotation from Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes to the effect that a page of history is worth a volume of logic. As psychoanalysts we have long been taught that hysterical patients suffer mainly from reminiscences. All mankind is victim of its own history. Analysts are no exceptions. In this presentation I propose to reflect in a psychoanalytic way on certain dilemmas in psychoanalytic education, dilemmas which indicate how history functions as a persistent dimension of our efforts as teachers of psychoanalysis.

Historical considerations were indeed in the forefront of the thinking of those who proposed and pursued the survey of psychoanalytic education and who helped organize COPE. These factors of history may be discussed under four headings.

1. The cultural lag

2. The ecology of institutes

3. Recurrent splits in institutes

4. The role of the training analyst

At a previous plenary session devoted to psychoanalytic education I commented on the relation between philosophy and practice in conducting programs of psychoanalytic training. Statements of principles boldly enunciated in the official catalogues or brochures of the institutes are not at all reliable indicators of the philosophy guiding the educational program. The real philosophy is empirically determined; it becomes eloquently clear in the institutional practices, in the organization, and in the implementation of the curriculum.

Submitted May 31, 1972

556

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Dream of the Marquis De Sade—Psychoanalytic Reflections on Narcissistic Trauma, Decompensation, and the Reconstitution of a Delusional Self11

Sheldon Bach Ph.D. and Lester Schwartz M.D.

Lester Schwartz

Sheldon Bach, Ph.D.
Lester Schwartz, M.D.
365 West End Avenue
New York, New York

Lester Schwartz  

SUMMARY

In this paper we examine a dream of the Marquis de Sade and a series of perverse fantasies from one of his major works, viewing them both as attempts to cope with narcissistic decompensation. We suggest that the public image of the Marquis de Sade is a manifestation

473

of a "delusional grandiose self," constructed in a restitutive attempt to recathect a traumatically disrupted childhood narcissistic self-object.

On the basis of a biographical fragment, we offer some speculations about the nature of this traumatic disruption and the restitutional attempts that follow. We relate these ideas to current concepts of narcissistic pathology and find them particularly congruent with the formulations advanced by Kohut.

We illustrate the unique emphasis that Sade places on anal fantasies and coprophagia, and we comment on the special relationship of the anal stage both to processes of idealization and disillusionment and to the formation of the narcissistic self-object.

In the context of Sade's psychosis, his narcissistic pathology, and the struggle to master his narcissistic rage, we view the masochistic fantasies as attempts to restitute delusionally idealized self-objects, and the sadistic fantasies as efforts to animate a delusional grandiose self. We see the sexualization of these fantasies as an attempt to deny experiences of self-fragmentation, bodily disruption, and "death of the self." We present a series of fantasies about murder and death, and we explore their relationship to narcissistic trauma, restitution and triumph.

A Study of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety. I. the Development of Freud's Theory of Anxiety

Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton, M.D.
416 North Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills, California 90210

Allan Compton  

SUMMARY

A study of the development of Freud's theory of anxiety has been presented, with emphasis on the relation of the anxiety theory to the clinical and general theoretical context of each phase of his work. The propositions involved in Freud's final formulations on anxiety are organized and summarized, and an attempt is made to integrate or eliminate earlier hypotheses, depending on their consistency with the eventual formulations. The effects of the discharge theory of affect are demonstrated in an effort to lay the groundwork for a more comprehensive psychoanalytic theory of anxiety within Freud's general framework.

Eugene Halpert, M.D.
245 East 87 St.
New York, N.Y. 10028

Eugene Halpert  

SUMMARY

The use of insurance for payment of analytic fee, particularly when the entire fee is paid this way, serves as a source of resistance. The unconscious fantasy is that "I am protected against loss of my penis and my body contents. I am in control and need not fear the analyst." Analysands in whom isolation and intellectualization are already favored mechanisms of defense and in whom anal level conflicts and fixations predominate may well be rendered unanalyzable when the entire analytic fee is paid by insurance.

Fabian X. Schupper, M.D.

New Brunswick, N. J. 08903

Fabian Schupper  

SUMMARY

In describing the various origins of Freud's formulations of the Oedipus complex, we first traced the entire development of his concept of the Oedipus complex. Next, we pointed out the opposition to Freud's theory, placing special emphasis on the revisions and modifications advocated by Jones, and the views of the major female analysts during the years following the introduction of the structural theory. Finally, we described the status of the Oedipus complex at the time of Freud's death.

[[RunningHead]]

The Experience of Time

John Kafka M.D.

John Kafka

John S. Kafka, M.D.
5323 Connecticut Ave. N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20015

John Kafka  

Hans Loewald introduced the panel, commenting on the remarkable fact that this was our first panel on this subject. It takes considerable courage to deal with the elusive concept of time, which is, perhaps, incomprehensible to the human mind. Analytic contributions, despite some increase in recent years, have in any case been relatively scarce. Freud experienced frustration in his attempts to deal with the concept of time, and for Whitehead, meditation on time underlined the limitations of human intelligence. In psychoanalysis as a treatment procedure, as a research method, and as a body of theory, however, concepts and phenomena related to time are essential. Memory, forgetting, regression, repetition, anticipation, presentation, representation, the influence on the present of the past in terms of thought, feeling, and behavior; delay of gratification, sleep-wakefulness rhythms, variations and abnormalities in the sense of elapsed time; the so-called timelessness of the id; values, standards, ideals as future-oriented categories; concepts such as object constancy and self identity—all these are central in our work. Add to this the consideration of time in the psychoanalytic process, such as schedules and the ending of hours. Furthermore, psychoanalysis is unthinkable without the theory of evolution and ontogenesis of mental development. It is in the mainstream of contemporary science where the equal importance of the historical dimension in the natural and the social sciences is now recognized. In this context, Loewald referred to Toulmin and Goodfield's book, The Discovery of Time.1 Fixations, delays, detours, arrests, and developmental spurts are major factors in shaping mental life and its disturbances. Emotional reworking of developmental factors in the present leads to more harmonious, less disturbed integration of personality and thus, to some degree, to mastery over the shape of the present and of the future.

THIS REPORT TO THE FELLOWS of the Board on Professional Standards closes my term as Chairman of the Committee on Institutes. My intent is to transmit a distillation of impressions and convictions about the institutes of the American Psychoanalytic Association and their educational processes. The formulations are primarily but not exclusively based on experience gained by site visits to various institutes. Some impressions are derived from statistical data solicited from those institutes which were visited. The other activities (workshops) of the Committee on Institutes also contributed to the development of these ideas.2

Before the visit itself, every statistical aspect of an institute was studied. In addition, minutes of meetings of all committees were carefully scrutinized by the subcommittee. Although the Committee's visit to an institute is relatively brief, it is astonishing how much is usually learned. Both the visitors and institutes were startled by this fact. At the beginning of a visit, an institute often will assume an attitude of "knowing superiority," implying that

Submitted Nov. 1, 1971

2These formulations and impressions are those of the author and not an expression of the entire Committee. The author wishes to take this opportunity to express his acknowledgement and appreciation of the perceptive and evocative contributions made to his understanding and awareness by the dedicated colleagues who constituted the Committee during the period in which these observations were made. Also, during this period, the guidance offered initially by Dr. Martin Stein, and then by Dr. Jacob Arlow as Chairman of the Board On Professional Standards, was of incomparable advantage. During this period, the author participated in seven of the twelve site visits. He was the Chairman of the Committee during three of these institute visits. The reader is referred to Dr. Royden C. [[RunningHead]]

Bridges—On Anticipation, Duration, and Meaning

René Spitz M.D.

René Spitz

René A. Spitz, M.D.
University of Colorado Medical Center
4200 East Ninth Avenue
Denver, Colorado 80220

René Spitz  

MR. CHAIRMAN, LADIES AND GENTLEMEN, MY DEAR FRIENDS:

Twelve years ago you honored me by entrusting me with the Freud Anniversary Lecture at the New York Academy of Medicine. On that occasion I dared greatly and presented a systematic review of my scientific position in respect to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic theory as a holistic developmental psychophysiological approach. It was published under the title: A Genetic Field Theory of Ego Formation. Essentially it was based, on the one hand, on my understanding of Freud's and Hartmann's theoretical position, on the other, on my own research findings.

What I will present to you now are tentative ideas: speculations which have occupied my mind for a number of years: adventures and experiments of the mind which I am trying out, to see if they have merit—and what better audience could I find for such an attempt than this one?

Of necessity my approach will have to be a multidimensional one. At no later period of life do so many different levels of the personality meet for the first time as in infancy. I will take birth as my starting point—a starting point with nodal qualities. For at this point the newborn is at the parting of the waters, a being about whom we know a great deal retrospectively but very little about the enormous potentialities he is bringing with him.

Address given on May 1, 1971 at the Plenary Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, D.C.

Submitted Sept. 8, 1972

721

The Pacifist Turn: An Episode of Mystic Illumination in the Autobiography of Bertrand Russell

Bennett Simon M.D. and Nancy Simon R.N.

Nancy Simon

Bennett Simon, M.D.
Nancy Simon, R.N.
16 Sedgemeadow Road
Wayland, Mass. 01778

Nancy Simon  

SUMMARY

We present an analysis of Bertrand Russell's account of an episode of "mystic illumination" which culminated in his "conversion" to pacifism. Our construction links the form and content of the episode, as well as its outcome, to the traumatic experience of early parental loss. We particularly emphasize the role of aggression engendered in the setting of separation and object loss as crucial to understanding Russell's pacifist turn.

Learning Theory and Psychoanalytic Theory

Gerald Aronson M.D.

Gerald Aronson

Gerald Aronson, M.D.
2080 Century Park East
Los Angeles, Calif. 9006

Gerald Aronson  

In his opening remarks, George Pollock expressed the hope for interdisciplinary integration of learning theories and psychoanalytic theories. He pointed out that because of time limitations and the present state of the art, a full discussion of the interrelationships of these theories would not be possible at this time and suggested a workshop in the future.

Pollock referred to his role as Chairman as a replacement for Gerhart Piers who had been ill. He summarized a contribution by Gerhart and Maria Piers1 on Modes of Learning and the Analytic Process, and he reviewed the three basic modes of learning in the analytic process: conditioning, insight learning, and learning through identification. Quoting the Piers' paper, Pollock spoke of conditioning as characterized by repetition, automatization, and a degree of dysphoria; insight learning by sudden closure and ego gratification; and identification, as distinguished from imitation, playing its part as a legitimate aspect of therapeutic analysis. The three modes of learning were viewed as possessing affinity to different ego structures and characterized by developmental phase specificity.

Further comment by Pollock, in step with the Piers' contribution, centered on three aspects of insight: insight connects motivation and behavior, diminishes the isolation between emotion and cognition, and loosens the repression barrier between past and present. These facets of insight are facilitated, or produced, by transference interpretation, confrontation, and genetic interpretations.

Held at the Fall Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 1971, Chairman: George H. Pollock, M.D.

Submitted May 30, 1972

1Piers, G. & Piers, M. (1964).622

The Years of Cope: Perspectives in Psychoanalytic Education—Introduction

Kenneth Calder M.D.

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth T. Calder, M.D.
110 East 78th Street
New York, New York 10021

Kenneth Calder  

IN 1959 THE PLANNING COMMITTEE FOR COPE recommended to the Board on Professional Standards that COPE be established to "provide consultation, initiate studies of various problems, collect data, pool information, conduct conferences, and carry on other activities deemed appropriate by the Board." A general goal for COPE was to educate the educators.

This goal need not be defended, but perhaps its importance should be emphasized. It seems self-evident that what we teach and how we teach it is of great concern to all psychoanalytic educators. Psychoanalysts, and especially psychoanalysts who teach, however, are extraordinarily busy. We have too little time to evaluate what we do. Furthermore, as educators we are amateurs. We are not trained or experienced in thinking about teaching goals and methods. We need all the help we can get; hence our special interest in consultations, studies, and the pooling of information designed to improve psychoanalytic education.

Some information about what COPE has done and how COPE now functions may be of interest and of aid in reading the papers that follow. COPE has now been in existence for slightly more than 10 years. In that time COPE has met as a Central Committee 23 times, and has influenced the publications of 10 or more papers or books.

I shall first review what COPE has done in the past 10 years.

Plenary presentation to the American Psychoanalytic Association by the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education (COPE) on April 30, 1971, in Washington, D.C.

[[RunningHead]]

Levels of Confidentiality in the Psychoanalytic Situation

Andrew Watson M.D.

Andrew Watson

Andrew S. Watson, M.D.
Children's Psychiatric Hospital University Hospital
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Andrew Watson  

David Beres opened the panel, commenting that although the title relates to the issue of confidentiality, it may be expected that our discussion will also consider issues of privilege and privacy as they relate to psychoanalysis.

He noted that the topics would fall into three general categories. First, the specific problems arising in the psychoanalytic situation: "Does the psychoanalytic situation present unique problems? What are the differences and similarities in the psychoanalytic vis-à-vis the psychotherapeutic situation? Are psychoanalytic records subject to subpoena? What problems arise when the psychoanalyst, who may be protected legally in some states, sends a report to a referring physician who does not have the right of privilege, as in a case in Connecticut? What about letters to draft boards, insurance companies, or other third party interests? What will happen if psychoanalysis is included in a possible national insurance bill? To what extent is confidentiality threatened in case discussions, published papers, and in tape recordings used for teaching or research? What are the problems in research situations involving a group of investigators? What are the special problems in analysis of children and adolescents?

"A most knotty problem arises in regard to the candidates in psychoanalysis. Do they as well as other patients retain privilege in communications to educational committees, the membership committee of the American, or other institute administrative bodies? What about confidentiality in the evaluation of persons considered for selection as training analysts?

"A second issue that we will be able to consider is what is now known as the Lifschutz Case." Beres noted that the presence of Dr. Lifschutz would turn the panel into something of a case seminar, inasmuch as his experiences were to be described.

"Third, there are general questions of the legal aspects vis-à-vis the ethical and professional aspects.

The Relationship of Language Development to Problem-Solving Ability

Henry Edelheit M.D.

Henry Edelheit

Henry Edelheit, M.D.
1140 Fifth Avenue
New York, New York 10028

Henry Edelheit  

Victor H. Rosen, opening the panel, noted that the cultural and psychological characteristics of modern man are inseparable from his linguistic and inventive capacities. The capacity for predication (defined as "the capacity to symbolize transitive actions") is the crucial factor distinguishing human from nonhuman forms of communication. This capacity is manifested in the normal human child late in the first year of life or early in the second. It seems to coincide developmentally with the achievement of object permanence (in the Piagetian meaning of the term) and also with the purposeful manipulation of objects. It is far from clear, however, whether there is a causal relationship between the linguistic and the other developmental achievements. As Eric Lenneberg has pointed out, language ability (which is species specific for human beings) is a relatively independent capacity and need not correspond to intelligence (even nanocephalic dwarfs and mongoloids have language). Rosen observed that Freud, in defining thought as "trial action," did not specify the symbolic modes of representation used in that process. Such representations need not be verbal. The question then arises what symbolic modes, verbal or otherwise, are used in the problem-solving trial action, and what in the nature of the encoding process makes possible the comprehension of the solution and the transmission of that solution to others. Encoding may itself, Rosen said, be regarded as the solution to a problem—a problem in predication.

Henry Edelheit focused on the specificity of human language as a uniquely organized system of voluntarily produced symbols (Sapir). These symbols are in the first instance auditory. However, once they exist in the community in the form of speech, they can be transferred to many other modalities. The Methodology of Psychoanalytic Biography

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John E. Gedo, M.D.
233 E. Erie
Chicago, Ill. 60611

John Gedo  

Charles Kligerman defined the methodological focus of the Panel by contrasting it with that of its 1956 predecessor on the "Contribution of Psychoanalysis to the Biography of the Artist." This Panel aims to discuss the biographical methods of psychoanalysis for subjects in any field of activity and will not consider the ancillary issue of creativity. Kligerman expressed doubts about the content of the 1956 Panel on only three points: the unwarranted assumption of one specific psychological constellation for "the artist," excessive optimism about the capacity of ego psychology to solve the methodological dilemmas of analytic biography, and unacknowledged value judgments about narcissism as a pathological trait in the personality. In the intervening years, this Journal has published two excellent reviews on our topic (H. Kohut, 1962 and J. E. Mack, 1971).

Increasing emphasis on the genetic point of view in formulating psychoanalytic data has gradually led to a rapprochement between psychoanalytic biography and clinical work. Although free association may leave lacunae in our knowledge of the analysand's functioning in conflict-free areas, proper assessment of his personality demands the reconstruction of the vicissitudes of multiple developmental lines in a manner which can correlate these with both psychic reality and the actualities. Analytic biography should aim for a similarly rounded picture of its subject's mental life.

Every biography worthy of the name, starting with those of Plutarch, has given a meaningful account of the subject's personality and motivations; great biographers have succeeded in making sensitive psychological estimations. These have to be based on some, albeit unarticulated, theory of personality; as psychoanalytic personality theory is the most profound, it has in fact become the standard utilized in the field of biography—no other psychological approach now receives serious consideration from scholars in

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association. New York City, December, 1971. Chairman: Charles Kligerman, M.D.

A Study of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety. II. Developments in the Theory of Anxiety Since 1926

Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton, M.D.
416 No. Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills, California 90210

Allan Compton  

SUMMARY

We have continued the study of the psychoanalytic theory of anxiety, taking up contributions since "Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety" in 1926, exclusive of Freud's work. The same method of dissection of theories is continued, with special attention paid to the observational and theoretical bases and elaborations of the concept of energic anxiety, as well as alternatives to this concept. Both Freud's work and later contributions are summarized. Conclusions are concerned with the methodology of the psychoanalytic study of anxiety.

[[RunningHead]]

Modes of Representation of Thought

Mardi Horowitz M.D.

Mardi Horowitz

Mardi J. Horowitz, M.D.
Mt. Zion Hospital & Medical Center
1600 Divisadero Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Mardi Horowitz  

SUMMARY

Thought representation can be divided into enactive, image, and lexical modes. Each system of representation has intrinsic organizational properties, although organizational formats developed with acquisition of one mode can be used in all other modes. Ordinarily, there is free translation of information between systems of representation. Neurological or psychological pathology may interfere with specific representational capacity or freedom of translation between modes of representation. By postulating controls at the boundaries of representational systems, one can specify the cognitive operations involved in defensive mechanisms. Use of the proposed model would allow precise description of conscious experience, especially of such global terms as "regression."

OUR DISCUSSION TODAY ON THE PAST, PRESENT, AND FUTURE of psychoanalytic education receives its impetus from two sources. The first is the tenth anniversary of COPE, marking the 10 years of national institutional attention by psychoanalysts to their functioning as educators, a decade of self-conscious search and study and of growth and maturation in this role qua educator, of which we can be duly proud. The second, the more powerful and even more compelling instigator of our exploration here together, is the searching, and in some ways revolutionary, reassessment from its lowest to its most advanced levels, that pervades the entire general educational enterprise today, and that indeed is raising fundamental questions about every aspect of even the most entrenched of the traditions and assumptions that characterize it.

For example, serious social critics, such as Paul Goodman (1964), in assessing the implications of the decline in IQ's that occurs in the course of ghetto schooling raises such fundamental questions about sacrosanct institutions as whether formal schooling (what he calls our system of "compulsory mis-education") is necessary at all, since those children could hardly do worse without it. [[RunningHead]]

"Of Music, Magic and Mystery": Studies in Adolescent Synthesis

Henry Rosner M.D.

Henry Rosner

Henry Rosner, M.D.
40 East 89th Street
New York, New York 10028

Henry Rosner  

ALTHOUGH FREUD (1905) published his case study of Dora in 1905, his analytic work with her took place in 1900. Thus, 1970 was the seventieth anniversary of the attempt to apply analytic treatment to adolescents. If Freud was not therapeutically successful, his insights from the experience—those pertaining to the problems of transference, for example—constituted landmarks in the history of psychoanalysis.

In spite of these insights, theoretical questions and problems of analytic management of the adolescent patient exist today. In fact, some still doubt whether analysis is even possible during the height of the adolescent upheaval, the phase under discussion in this paper. Blos (1962) called this period of development adolescence proper, whereas Deutsch (1944) referred to it as mid-adolescence. Briefly, it is the moment of development when oedipal conflicts are revived, reworked, and more or less resolved. Incestuous ties to the parents are renounced, and there is a restructuring of all three parts of the psychic apparatus. The result is an expanded ego organization and a more flexible superego that accepts the new phase-specific drive derivative, genitality.

Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Division of Psychoanalytic Education, Department of Psychiatry, Downstate Medical Center, Brooklyn, New York.

First presented at the Fifth Annual Scientific Meeting of The American Association for Child Psychoanalysis, Inc., Hershey, Pennsylvania, April 7, 1970. Subsequently presented to the Psychoanalytic Association of New York, November 16, 1970; The Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic, London, February 24, 1971; and the New York Psychoanalytic Society, March 30, 1971.

[[RunningHead]]

Aggression: A View of Theory Building in Psychoanalysis

Albert Solnit M.D.

Albert Solnit

Albert J. Solnit, M.D.
333 Cedar Street
New Haven, Conn 06510

Albert Solnit  

SUMMARY

The postulation of the theory of aggression as an instinctual drive has been productive in illuminating vicissitudes of child development and behavior. Observations from psychoanalytic treatment, from nonpsychoanalytic settings and from experiences in applying psychoanalytic theory in the service of educating and caring for children can elaborate and refine the psychoanalytic theory of aggression. Such elaborations and refinements suggest trends in theory building in psychoanalysis and can lead to new hypotheses. At the same time, psychoanalytic theory can be useful in improving services for children.

The resonances that can be set up between theory building and expository views of a child's behavior are particularly enriching and vital for the elaboration of our science. This trend also

449

can lead to a clarification of methodological issues in psychoanalytic and child-development research. In this paper I use the study of aggression as a current model to demonstrate this productive resonance.

responseSet.title: Freud's Conception of the Negative Therapeutic Reaction, with Comments on Instinct Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Conception of the Negative Therapeutic Reaction, with Comments on Instinct Theory

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans W. Loewald, M.D.
65 Trumbull Street,
New Haven, Conn. 06510

Hans Loewald  

SUMMARY

Freud's conception of the negative therapeutic reaction, as outlined and elaborated in some of his later writings, is re-examined, especially in terms of some of its theoretical implications regarding instinct theory. A reformulation of the concept of instinctual drives is proposed. The negative therapeutic reaction is conceived by Freud as the manifestation of self-destructive tendencies which are not primarily a by-product of reactions against the therapist and his efforts but stem from a deeply rooted intrapsychic imbalance between libidinal and aggressive drives (or of the life and death instincts). This imbalance in favor of the destructive drives may appear in the form of unconscious guilt feelings (superego versus ego) and pervasive masochistic tendencies, or in the more primitive and intractable form of unstructured ("unbound") destructive forces which have not attained sufficient psychic representation to be assignable to the superego as a mental province. In this insidious form the negative therapeutic reaction must be attributed to distorted organization of the instinctual drives themselves, prior to their structuralization in psychic subsystems. It is in this sense that Freud ultimately traces the negative therapeutic reaction back to a prevalence of the destructive drive or death instinct in the economy of mental life. The presence of an unconscious sense of guilt already presupposes the binding of such destructive forces in the subsystem superego.

In its more intractable forms the negative therapeutic reaction is rooted in preoedipal, primitive distortions of instinctual and ego development and is thus hardly amenable to interpretations in terms of guilt, conscience, and need for punishment.

In this connection a revision of the instinct concept itself is proposed. It is suggested that instinctual drives, understood as psychic forces, are to be conceptualized as becoming organized through interactions within the primitive mother-child unitary

244

psychic field rather than as constitutional or innate givens. This would imply that the prevalence of self-destructive forces has something to do with particular interactions with the primitive environment; that organism and environment in their interactions and differentiation from one another produce, as it were, the individual's life and death instincts and their relative proportions; their origins and vicissitudes are not primarily independent of the caring persons. Such a view of the organization of instinctual drives would accord with the clinical impression that severe and ingrained cases of negative therapeutic reaction are rooted in extreme preoedipal disturbances of primitive object-ties.

[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Assessment of Structural Change

Paul Dewald M.D.

Paul Dewald

Paul A. Dewald, M.D.
4524 Forest Park
St. Louis, Missouri 63108

Paul Dewald  

SUMMARY

Although the production of structural change in the personality is commonly accepted as a goal of psychoanalytic treatment, there is relatively little mention in the analytic literature of specific criteria by which such change is to be assessed. Attempts have been made to describe the process of structural change as it occurs in psychoanalysis and to differentiate such changes from those occurring in other forms of therapy. Structural change is described as a process occurring throughout the entire course of analysis, but a distinction is made regarding the change in derivative vs. core psychic structures. The ongoing process of assessment of structural change is described, and clinical illustrations of change are presented, in an attempt to illustrate and define the process more accurately.

[[RunningHead]]

Time as a Dimension of Affects

Peter Hartocollis M.D.

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis, M.D.
C. F. Menninger Memorial Hospital
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Peter Hartocollis  

SUMMARY

Everyday and clinical experience suggest that the sense of time, what is known as psychological or experiential time, is an essential factor in the determination of the quality of affects.

In order to feel either anxiety or depression, one must first perceive a given situation as dangerous, and then assess oneself as inadequate. The result is a painful tension, but not yet anxiety or depression. What determines whether this tension—felt as a decrease in self-esteem or as a state of helplessness—will assume the specific quality of anxiety or depression is the individual's orientation in time. When perceiving the noxious influence—in essence, inadequacy—as something in the future, only potential and therefore avoidable, one experiences anxiety. When perceiving oneself as inadequate in the past, one experiences depression.

Judging oneself as inadequate while being unable to place the inadequacy in a time perspective, future or past, results in a sense of impatience with the present; and the overall experience is that of boredom. On the positive side, affects like joy or elation occur when the person judges himself adequate rather than inadequate with reference to some future or past, real or imaginary, event. An ecstatic, nirvana-like experience, induced by certain

106

drugs and characterizing the condition of a person who is in love and feels loved, obtains when the individual perceives himself as adequate in the absence of any concern for time, future or past. In general, affects, painful and pleasurable alike, can be placed into a cognitive relationship involving subjective adequacy along the continuum of experiential time.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on Transference: Universal Phenomenon and Hardest Part of Analysis

Brian Bird M.D.

Brian Bird

Brian Bird, M.D.
11328 Euclid Avenue
Cleveland, Ohio 44106

Brian Bird  

AS AN INTRODUCTION I WOULD LIKE to make a few general remarks about transference as I see it. Transference, in my view, is a very special mental quality that has never been satisfactorily explained. I am not satisfied, for instance, either with what has been written about it or with its use in analysis. To me, our knowledge seems slight, and our use limited. This view, admittedly extreme, is possible only because transference is such a very remarkable phenomenon, with a great and largely undeveloped potential. I am particularly taken with the as yet unexplored idea that transference is a universal mental function which may well be the basis of all human relationships. I even suspect it of being one of the mind's main agencies for giving birth to new ideas, and new life to old ones. In these several respects, transference would seem to me to assume characteristics of a major ego function.

I tend to go along with those who consider transference unique as it occurs in the analytic situation, and with those who hold that the analysis and resolution of a transference neurosis is the only avenue to the farthest reaches of the mind. It is also my belief that transference, in one form or another, is always present, active, and significant in the analytic situation. From this it should follow that rarely is there a need to give up on the transference or to doubt that everything that goes on in analysis has a transference meaning. I would also be inclined to agree with those, perhaps few in number, who harbor the idea that analysts themselves regularly develop transference reactions to their patients, including periods of transference neurosis, and that these transference reactions play an essential role in the analytic process.

Submitted June 28, 1971

[[RunningHead]]

The Birth of Cope as Viewed in 1971

Joan Fleming M.D.

Joan Fleming

Joan Fleming, M.D.
University of Colorado Medical Center
4200 E. Ninth Avenue
Denver, Colo. 80220

Joan Fleming  

TALKING TO YOU ABOUT THE BIRTH OF THE COMMITTEE on Psychoanalytic Education is a pleasure—not because it takes me back over a span of 20 years, but because so much has happened in that time. Many changes have taken place among ourselves and in the world in which we live, as analysts and as members of an analytic association. It is now time to review the events that brought COPE into being, to assess its activities in relation to its original aims, and to consider the direction we want to take in the future. It is not only to the present generation of psychoanalytic educators that I speak today, but to all the members of the Association from whose ranks the educators of the future will come. Theirs will be the responsibility for insuring the quality of our scientific growth by providing for increasing excellence in our educational programs.

To introduce what I have to say, let me offer you two texts that will better define my purpose today and my hopes for the future. The first is a remark by Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes, (Gregg, 1957, p. 1), "A page of history is worth a volume of logic." We need to find out what our history can teach us. The second is from Gregg himself, who describes the purpose of a professional association such as ours when he says, "What binds men together in effective association [italics mine] are three things: shared experiences, beliefs generally agreed upon, and hopes for the future held in common" (Gregg, 1957, p. 16).

Submitted May 31, 1972
